title: Juniper Networks PTX Series Router Critical Unauthenticated Root RCE via On-Box Anomaly Detection Framework Misconfiguration Enables Complete Network Infrastructure Takeover (CVE-2026-21902, CVSS 9.8)
affected_products: Juniper Networks PTX Series Routers running affected Junos OS Evolved versions

sources.name: BleepingComputer - Critical Juniper PTX Flaw Allows Full Router Takeover
SecurityWeek - Juniper Networks PTX Routers Affected by Critical Vulnerability
Security Affairs - Juniper Issues Emergency Patch for Critical PTX Router RCE
VPNCentral - Juniper PTX Routers Patched Against Root RCE
Cyber Security Agency of Singapore - Alert AL-2026-020
Canadian Centre for Cyber Security - Juniper Advisory AV26-172
Threat Intelligence Report - Critical Juniper PTX Flaw

timeline.event: Juniper Networks internally discovers the vulnerability in On-Box Anomaly Detection framework on PTX Series routers running Junos OS Evolved
Juniper Networks publishes security advisory JSA107128 and releases patches for affected Junos OS Evolved versions
Multiple government CERTs (Singapore CSA, Canadian CCCS) issue advisories warning of the critical vulnerability
Security researchers publish detailed technical analyses and proof-of-concept discussions highlighting the trivial exploitation complexity
No evidence of in-the-wild exploitation reported yet, but exploitation expected given CVSS 9.8 score and low attack complexity

simple_summary.what_happened: A critical vulnerability was discovered in Juniper Networks PTX Series routers, which are high-performance core and edge network devices used by Internet Service Providers, telecommunications companies, and large enterprises to route massive volumes of network traffic. The flaw exists in the On-Box Anomaly Detection framework, a built-in service that monitors for unusual network behavior. Due to incorrect permission settings, this service is accidentally exposed to the external network on a default port without any authentication requirement. Anyone who can reach the router over the network can send a single specially crafted packet to execute commands with full root (administrator) privileges, completely taking over the device.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running Juniper Networks Junos OS Evolved version 25.4R1-EVO (before 25.4R1-S1-EVO) or 25.4R2-EVO on PTX Series routers. Standard Junos OS (non-Evolved) is NOT affected, and versions before 25.4R1-EVO are also NOT affected. PTX Series routers are primarily used by ISPs, telecommunications providers, data center operators, and large enterprises as core and edge routing platforms handling high-volume network traffic.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: The attacker identifies a Juniper PTX Series router on the network through port scanning or targeting known ISP and telecommunications infrastructure that commonly deploys these routers. Step 2: The attacker probes for and discovers the externally exposed anomaly detection service port, which should only be accessible internally but is incorrectly exposed by default. Step 3: The attacker sends a specially crafted network packet to the anomaly detection service endpoint, exploiting the incorrect permission assignment to bypass all access controls. Step 4: The anomaly detection framework, running with root privileges, executes the attacker's injected code without any authentication or authorization validation, granting immediate root-level access. Step 5: With complete root control of the PTX router, the attacker can install persistent backdoors, modify routing tables to redirect or intercept all network traffic, exfiltrate sensitive data flowing through the router, and pivot to other network devices and segments.
what_you_should_do: IMMEDIATE: Identify all PTX Series routers running Junos OS Evolved and determine current firmware versions. Prioritize internet-facing and edge routers for assessment
Apply patches urgently: Upgrade to Junos OS Evolved 25.4R1-S1-EVO, 25.4R2-EVO, 26.2R1-EVO, or any subsequent version

If immediate patching is not possible, disable the anomaly detection service as a temporary mitigation using 'request pfe anomalies disable' and implement firewall ACLs to block external access to the service port

Monitor PTX routers for signs of compromise: unexpected outbound connections, configuration changes outside maintenance windows, new user accounts, and abnormal traffic patterns originating from router interfaces
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: PTX Series routers sit at the core of major network infrastructure, handling massive volumes of traffic for ISPs, telecommunications companies, and large enterprises. A compromised core router gives attackers a strategic vantage point to intercept, redirect, or modify all network traffic flowing through it, including sensitive corporate communications, financial transactions, and government data. With a CVSS score of 9.8 and an attack that requires no authentication, no user interaction, and minimal complexity, this vulnerability is trivially exploitable once a target is identified. While no in-the-wild exploitation has been confirmed yet, the combination of critical severity and easy exploitation makes it a prime target for nation-state actors and sophisticated threat groups.

tactics.description: Unauthenticated remote code execution via externally exposed anomaly detection service on PTX routers that should only be internally accessible
Arbitrary command execution with root privileges through the exploited anomaly detection framework service
Attacker with root access can modify router firmware to maintain persistence across reboots and updates
Attacker can alter the router operating system image to hide malicious modifications and maintain stealthy presence
Compromised core router enables interception and analysis of all network traffic flowing through the device
Root access to the PTX router enables pivoting to adjacent network segments and other infrastructure devices

detection_guidance.summary: Detection should focus on monitoring for unauthorized connections to the anomaly detection service port, unexpected command execution with root privileges on PTX routers, abnormal outbound connections from router management interfaces, and unauthorized configuration changes. Since no in-the-wild exploitation has been confirmed yet, proactive monitoring and rapid patching are the primary defensive strategies.

log_sources.description: Monitor for unexpected process execution, configuration changes, and authentication anomalies on PTX routers
Track connections to the anomaly detection service port from external sources and unusual outbound connections from PTX router management interfaces
Alert on routing table modifications, ACL changes, and user account creation that occur outside documented maintenance windows
Monitor for exploitation attempts targeting the anomaly detection service port and flag connection attempts from unauthorized sources

what_to_look_for.indicator: External connections to anomaly detection service port
what_to_look_for.description: The anomaly detection framework should only accept internal connections. Any external connection attempts indicate reconnaissance or exploitation
what_to_look_for.log_field: netflow: dst_port=<anomaly_detection_port> AND src_ip NOT IN (internal_ranges)

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unexpected process execution with root privileges on PTX routers
what_to_look_for.description: Exploitation results in arbitrary code execution as root. Monitor for unusual processes spawned by the anomaly detection service
what_to_look_for.log_field: syslog: process_name='anomaly_detection' AND child_process NOT IN (expected_children)

what_to_look_for.description: Compromised routers may show routing table modifications, new user accounts, or ACL changes made during non-maintenance periods
what_to_look_for.log_field: config_log: commit_time NOT IN (maintenance_windows) AND user NOT IN (authorized_admins)

what_to_look_for.indicator: Abnormal outbound connections from router management interfaces
what_to_look_for.description: A compromised PTX router may establish connections to external command-and-control infrastructure or attempt lateral movement to internal networks
what_to_look_for.log_field: netflow: src_ip=<ptx_mgmt_ip> AND dst_ip NOT IN (known_management_hosts)

immediate_actions.action: Inventory all PTX Series routers running Junos OS Evolved
immediate_actions.description: Identify all PTX Series routers in your environment and document their current Junos OS Evolved version. Focus on devices running version 25.4R1-EVO or 25.4R2-EVO. Prioritize internet-facing and edge-deployed routers.

immediate_actions.action: Apply security patches to affected PTX routers
immediate_actions.description: Upgrade to Junos OS Evolved 25.4R1-S1-EVO, 25.4R2-EVO (patched build), or 26.2R1-EVO. Follow Juniper's upgrade procedures and test in staging environment if possible. Schedule emergency maintenance windows for production routers.

immediate_actions.action: Disable anomaly detection service as temporary mitigation
immediate_actions.description: If immediate patching is not feasible, disable the On-Box Anomaly Detection framework to prevent exploitation. This removes the attack surface but also disables network anomaly monitoring capabilities.

immediate_actions.action: Implement network access controls on management interfaces
immediate_actions.description: Configure firewall ACLs or Junos filter rules to restrict access to PTX router management interfaces and the anomaly detection service port to authorized management networks only. Block all untrusted external access.

immediate_actions.action: Monitor for exploitation attempts and compromise indicators
immediate_actions.description: Set up alerts for external connections to the anomaly detection service port, unexpected configuration changes, new user account creation, and abnormal outbound traffic from PTX router interfaces. Review logs for any suspicious activity since the vulnerability was introduced in version 25.4R1-EVO.
